category: path traversal / arbitrary native module load
title: User-supplied prebuiltPath/sourcePath passed directly to require() without sanitization, enabling arbitrary native addon load
description: loadNativeBinding() accepts NativeLoaderOptions where prebuiltPath and sourcePath are caller-controlled strings that are passed verbatim to Node's require(). A caller (or a consumer reading a path from untrusted input such as a config file or query parameter) can load an arbitrary .node native addon from any filesystem path, including network shares or temp directories. There is no extension check, no canonical-path check, and no allowlist. The only guard is ensureBindingLoaded(), which checks the loaded module's version string — a crafted malicious addon can easily pass that check.

category: decompression bomb / missing input size cap
title: Default maxPixels of 1<<28 (268 million pixels) allows decompression bombs for callers who omit the field
description: createNativeCodecFacade().createDecoder() merges a default of maxPixels: 1 << 28 (268,435,456 pixels ≈ 16384×16384 at 4 bytes = ~1 GB of output). Any caller that does not explicitly pass a lower maxPixels will silently accept images up to that limit. A malicious JXL file with a large declared dimension can force the native decoder to allocate up to ~1 GB before the guard fires. The default should be much lower (e.g., 64 MP = 67,108,864) or the caller should be required to supply it explicitly.

title: Stub detection relies on fragile substring checks that can be bypassed by a crafted version string or probe path
description: ensureBindingLoaded() uses version().includes('scaffold') and probe.path.toLowerCase().includes('stub') to reject placeholder addons. These are bypassable: an attacker-supplied .node file can return any version string and any probe path. More practically, a legitimate .node built into a path containing the word 'stub' in its directory name would be incorrectly rejected. The check is also case-sensitive for 'scaffold' but case-insensitive for 'stub', creating inconsistency. Neither check constitutes a real integrity guarantee.

category: no validation of native escape hatch inputs
title: advancedFrameSettings ids and values are forwarded to native C++ without any range or allowlist check
description: normalizeEncoderOptions() collects advancedFrameSettings entries (caller-supplied id/value pairs) and merges them with convenience-field-derived entries before forwarding to the native addon. No check is performed on id values or value ranges before they reach the C++ JxlEncoderFrameSettingSetFloatValue / JxlEncoderFrameSettingSetValue calls. Depending on how the native layer handles out-of-range ids or extreme values, this could trigger undefined behavior or crashes in libjxl. An attacker-controlled EncoderOptions (e.g., from a parsed config) could supply crafted id/value pairs.

category: secrets / developer paths in committed test code
title: Hardcoded absolute developer machine paths committed to version control expose internal directory layout
description: Lines 5-6 hardcode absolute Windows paths (C:\Foo\raw-converter\target\release\build\jpegxl-sys-26f294f2024eaecb\out\include and C:\TEMP\jxl-mt-libs) as fallback defaults when env vars are absent. These paths reveal the developer's local machine layout, internal build artifact hash (26f294f2024eaecb), and temp directory convention. While these are test-only and env-var overrides exist, the hardcoded values are committed to the repo and will appear in CI logs, diffs, and clones, disclosing filesystem topology.

title: guardEncoderOptions is intentionally empty — there is no validation convention for EncoderOptions fields passed to native
description: guardDecoderOptions() validates region bounds but guardEncoderOptions() is an empty stub with a comment noting no guards remain. Width, height, distance, quality, effort, and all extra-channel fields are forwarded to native C++ without any JS-side range or type checks. Adding a lightweight validation pass (e.g., width/height > 0, effort in 1..9, distance in [0, 25], quality in [0, 100]) would catch misconfigured callers before reaching native code and provide consistent error messages rather than opaque native failures.
